body_txt: In fiction, robots have a tendency to act very silly when they've sustained damage (especially to the head ) or are defective, poorly programmed, running low on batteries, worn out, or otherwise not functioning as they should, like a human who just had a bad concussion. They start spouting random non-sequiturs or complete gibberish, start singing "The Star-Spangled Banner", stumble around in circles like a drunk or do other funny things. Logically speaking, there's no practical reason that a robot would be programmed to say "The waffle iron is in the toybox!" or start beating itself upside the head after being damaged, so chalk it up to Rule of Funny . This trope has aspects of Ridiculously Human Robots . Humans say and do funny things when they're tired, drunk, high, or concussed; therefore, cartoon logic dictates that a robot who thinks and acts like a human — even if they don't necessarily look like one — would do something similar when damaged. Most cases of this trope are limited to a brief gag or a Glitch Episode , but some robot characters act like this all the time because of some inherent defect in their programming. In which case, there will usually be an episode where they are fixed and start behaving as they should, but since Status Quo Is God , they’re right back to their old goofy self by the end of the episode or the beginning of the next. A subtrope of Funny Robot and sister trope to (and can overlap with) A.I. Is a Crapshoot . Related:
